It did the opposite.

For the first time in history, humans are cheaper than software.

tweets.text:
The internet economy is seeing historic growth.

Over the last decade, the way people build businesses has rapidly transitioned from brick-and-mortar to online.

With the growth of AI, this trend has only accelerated, and the digital economy is now officially valued at over $20 trillion.

The digital economy also now accounts for ~18% of  global GDP.

As a result, US business formation is experiencing a record-breaking boom, with over 3.1 million applications filed in the first half of 2026, up +17% YoY.

Today, more than 200 million people worldwide identify as creators, entrepreneurs, or online business owners.

AI continues to transform the digital economy.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:

tweets.text:
BREAKING: DeepSeek is preparing for an IPO and may file as soon as this year, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. The company is reportedly in talks with accounting and banking advisors and the IPO is expected to be listed in China

2. DeepSeek has begun talks for a fresh funding round valuing the company at $71 billion

3. Amid rumors of an IPO, DeepSeek is now looking to expand into the agentic AI market

The AI arms race is accelerating.

tweets.text:
BREAKING: The US Treasury posted a $120 billion deficit in June, a sharp deterioration from a $27 billion surplus in June 2025.

This comes as government receipts dropped -$31 billion YoY, to $496 billion.

The decline was largely driven by a surge in tariff refund payments after the Supreme Court ruled the tariff increases illegal, resulting in a -$26 billion net decline in customs duty receipts.

At the same time, outlays surged +$117 billion YoY, to $616 billion.

In the first 9 months of FY2026, the US deficit rose +$29 billion YoY, to $1.37 trillion, the 3rd-highest deficit at this point in the fiscal year in history.

Over the same period, net interest expense rose +$78 billion YoY, to $827 billion, on track for the highest annual net interest expense on record. 

The US deficit crisis is accelerating.
tweets.full_text:
BREAKING: The US Treasury posted a $120 billion deficit in June, a sharp deterioration from a $27 billion surplus in June 2025.

This comes as government receipts dropped -$31 billion YoY, to $496 billion.

The decline was largely driven by a surge in tariff refund payments after the Supreme Court ruled the tariff increases illegal, resulting in a -$26 billion net decline in customs duty receipts.

At the same time, outlays surged +$117 billion YoY, to $616 billion.

In the first 9 months of FY2026, the US deficit rose +$29 billion YoY, to $1.37 trillion, the 3rd-highest deficit at this point in the fiscal year in history.

Over the same period, net interest expense rose +$78 billion YoY, to $827 billion, on track for the highest annual net interest expense on record. 

The US deficit crisis is accelerating.

The greenback sits at 101.27, above both the 21- and 200-day.

Relative strength to Bitcoin keeps rising. 

RSI is stretched, but the breakout structure stays bullish

$VIX futures stay in risk-on contango. But the Jul-Aug spread's relative strength to the VIX has maxed out, so leveraged traders are fully invested, long stocks and short vol. New highs in the spread, fading daily RSI. 

That divergence is telling us that risk is building https://t.co/D9DwlF6zhv

tweets.text: On this day in 1789, a Paris mob stormed the Bastille, kicking off a revolution that was, at its root, a sovereign debt crisis. France had spent itself into insolvency helping finance the American Revolution, and by 1789 roughly half of all government revenue went to interest payments on 4 billion livres in accumulated debt. King Louis XVI had convened the Estates-General for the first time in 175 years because the state was running out of money and the aristocracy refused to accept new taxes. The revolution destroyed the fortunes of the French nobility, wiped out foreign creditors holding government bonds, and introduced the assignat, a paper currency backed by confiscated church lands that lost 97% of its value within six years. The revolutionary government's decision to default on the royal debt in 1797 locked France out of European capital markets for a generation.
tweets.full_text: On this day in 1789, a Paris mob stormed the Bastille, kicking off a revolution that was, at its root, a sovereign debt crisis. France had spent itself into insolvency helping finance the American Revolution, and by 1789 roughly half of all government revenue went to interest payments on 4 billion livres in accumulated debt. King Louis XVI had convened the Estates-General for the first time in 175 years because the state was running out of money and the aristocracy refused to accept new taxes. The revolution destroyed the fortunes of the French nobility, wiped out foreign creditors holding government bonds, and introduced the assignat, a paper currency backed by confiscated church lands that lost 97% of its value within six years. The revolutionary government's decision to default on the royal debt in 1797 locked France out of European capital markets for a generation.

tweets.text:
@fejau_inc I’d disagree with the entire characterization.

Waller laid out a v specific reaction function, which is a necessary part of central bank communication. This is nothing like the forward guidance that occurs at the zero bound.

thread_tweets.text:

thread_tweets.full_text:
@fejau_inc I’d disagree with the entire characterization.

Waller laid out a v specific reaction function, which is a necessary part of central bank communication. This is nothing like the forward guidance that occurs at the zero bound.

thread_tweets.text:
Updating your reaction function can have an impact on markets…it was far from a capital market disaster yesterday. Also, that cold of a cpi print would have moved markets no matter what Waller said.

The negative associated with forward guidance is inflexibility…Living in a world where every central banker sounds like warsh would be much more volatile over a more intermediate time frame imo
thread_tweets.full_text:

The negative associated with forward guidance is inflexibility…Living in a world where every central banker sounds like warsh would be much more volatile over a more intermediate time frame imo

thread_tweets.text:
@fejau_inc FWIW if I were to criticize Waller it would be that he expressed a significantly more hawkish reaction function compared to his May speech…not that he explicitly communicated it.

May seem like splitting hairs but I believe the distinction is important
thread_tweets.full_text:
@fejau_inc FWIW if I were to criticize Waller it would be that he expressed a significantly more hawkish reaction function compared to his May speech…not that he explicitly communicated it.

May seem like splitting hairs but I believe the distinction is important

thread_tweets.text:
Again, I don't think you understand the difference between defining a reaction function and making a forecast/forward guidance. They are not the same. And, you seem to use them interchangeably. This may come across as pedantic, but the distinction matters.

Waller did not make a forecast yesterday. He defined how he would react to incoming data. The room for critique is that this reaction (to the data) function changed too quickly. Whether this speech top ticked cpi data is inconsequential.

thread_tweets.text:
@chilicon73 There's an old saying among lawyers: "Never ask a question that you don't already know the answer to."

In central banking, the corollary is: "Never hinge policy on one datapoint that you don't already have in hand."

tweets.text:
First Principles Ep. 4 with Alvin Roth

Long before crypto made coordination programmable, Nobel Prize winner Alvin Roth was designing markets where coordination could save lives, matching doctors to hospitals, students to schools, and kidney donors to the patients who need them.

Roth explains why markets are not just natural forces, but engineered systems; why the details of timing, congestion, incentives, and trust can make or break a marketplace; and why some of the most important markets are the ones where simply exchanging money can’t do the work.

Hosted by @Tim_Roughgarden with @skominers.

00:00 Intro: Why market design matters
04:18 The economist as engineer
08:09 When theory meets the real world
07:02 Fixing the medical residency match
15:32 Why markets unravel
18:22 Redesigning NYC high school admissions
28:05 The hidden problem of congestion
34:47 How kidney exchange saves lives
45:26 How the internet changed market design
48:25 Airbnb, Uber and smarter marketplaces
51:28 Repugnant transactions and moral economics
53:32 When markets need social support
54:32 The unexpected effects of criminalizing surrogacy
01:04:58 Preference signals and the job market
01:18:53 A broken market: resettling refugees and other migrants

3rd week was red going into CPI.

Then Bitcoin bounced higher into late July/early August, before giving all the gains back by September.

Similar setup today. https://t.co/PAOaiEC2sq

tweets.text:
Natural Gas is entering a historically strong seasonal stretch.

Since 1985, holding only from TDY 139 through TDY 196 turned $1 into $44.86.

For 2026, that window runs from July 16 through October 6.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/0JRHYTSrAh https://t.co/tFt0tzsSRX

About the first part, I actually wonder if the algorithm driven networks can now surface a bit more diversity around the norms.
